title: Customize Pipeline Stage Colors in GoHighLevel — Visual Deal Management
description: Learn how to customize pipeline stage colors in GoHighLevel's Opportunities module. Step-by-step guide for color-coded deal management, Kanban views & best practices.
html_content:
Managing a sales pipeline without visual clarity is like flying blind. Your team wastes time hunting for deals, losing track of priorities, and missing critical stages. GoHighLevel's Opportunities module solves this with customizable pipeline stage colors — a game-changing feature that transforms how agencies organize and track deals.

In this guide, I'll walk you through every step of setting up color-coded pipelines in GoHighLevel, so your team can scan deal stages at a glance, stay focused, and close deals faster. Whether you're managing real estate, home services, or B2B sales, this visual system cuts through noise and keeps everyone aligned. Ready to streamline your workflow? Let's get started — and grab your free 30-day GoHighLevel trial to test everything hands-on.

📋 In This Guide

  • How to Access and Create Pipelines in GoHighLevel
  • Step-by-Step: Customizing Pipeline Stage Colors
  • How Color-Coded Stages Display Across Views
  • Best Practices for Color-Coding Pipeline Stages
  • Using Smart Tags to Enhance Pipeline Organization

How to Access and Create Pipelines in GoHighLevel

Before you can customize colors, you need to access the Opportunities module and either use an existing pipeline or create a new one. Here's the fastest route:

Navigate to the Opportunities dropdown: From your GoHighLevel dashboard, locate the Opportunities section in the main menu. This is your hub for all deal management.

Select or Create a Pipeline: If you're starting fresh, click the Pipelines option and hit + New Pipeline. Give it a clear name (e.g., "Sales Pipeline," "Real Estate Listings," or "Service Contracts").

Add Your Stages: Once your pipeline exists, you'll need to define stages. These are the steps deals move through — from Lead to Negotiation to Closed Won. You can add as many stages as your sales process requires, and each one will get its own color assignment.

The pipeline infrastructure in GoHighLevel is flexible enough to match any sales methodology, whether you're using a 3-stage process or a 10-stage funnel.

Step-by-Step: Customizing Pipeline Stage Colors

This is where the magic happens. Customizing stage colors in GoHighLevel takes seconds but creates massive visual impact on your team's workflow.

Step 1: Open Your Pipeline Settings
Navigate to the pipeline you want to customize. Click the Settings or Edit icon (typically a gear or pencil icon) next to the pipeline name.

Step 2: Access Stage Configuration
You'll see a list of all stages in your pipeline. Each stage will have an option to edit its properties, including its color assignment. Look for a color picker or stage settings button next to each stage name.

Step 3: Select Your Color
Click the color option for a stage. GoHighLevel provides a color palette with pre-defined options (reds, blues, greens, yellows, etc.). Choose colors that create intuitive visual hierarchy:

  • Early stages (Lead, Prospecting): Cool colors like blue or teal suggest early-stage interest
  • Mid-pipeline stages (Negotiation, Proposal): Warm colors like yellow or orange indicate movement and activity
  • Final stages (Closed Won, Closed Lost): Green for wins, red for losses — the universal visual language of sales

Step 4: Save Your Changes
Once you've assigned colors to all stages, click Save or Update Pipeline. Changes apply instantly across your entire CRM.

💡 Pro Tip

Consistency is key. Use the same color scheme across all your pipelines (if you manage multiple). This trains your team's muscle memory and makes them scan faster, even when switching between different deal types.

How Color-Coded Stages Display Across Views

One of GoHighLevel's strengths is flexibility in how you view your pipeline. Your custom colors work seamlessly across all three primary views:

Kanban View: This is the most visually impactful. Each stage appears as its own column, with deal cards stacked underneath. Your custom colors are immediately visible as column headers, giving your team instant visual feedback. Team members can drag deals between colored stages, and the color coding reinforces which pipeline stage they're moving into.

List View: For those who prefer tabular data, the list view displays stages as a column with color-coded badges or text. You can sort by stage, and the colors still provide quick visual scanning without the Kanban layout.

Individual Record View: When a team member opens a specific opportunity (deal), they see the stage name with its assigned color prominently displayed. This context is crucial when team members are deep in deal details and need to remember where this opportunity sits in the pipeline.

The consistency of color coding across all three views means your team develops a unified mental model of your pipeline — dramatically speeding up navigation and decision-making.

Best Practices for Color-Coding Pipeline Stages

Simply assigning colors isn't enough. Strategic color coding amplifies clarity. Here's how top agencies use it:

Match Your Sales Methodology: If you use a named methodology (like MEDDIC or Sandler), align colors with that framework. Your team will already think in those terms, so colors become intuitive reinforcement.

Use Contrast for Quick Scanning: Avoid similar colors for adjacent stages. A deal moving from light blue to slightly darker blue confuses. Jump from blue to orange to green. High contrast = faster recognition.

Consider Emotional Association: Red typically means urgency or loss. Green means go or win. Yellow means caution or pending. Use these psychological anchors intentionally.

Establish Color Rules Across the Team: Document your pipeline colors and their meaning in a team guide. New team members onboard faster when they know that "orange stage = proposal sent, expecting response by Friday."

Test Before Rolling Out: Run your color scheme on a test pipeline for a week. Ask team members for feedback. Adjust before applying to your primary pipelines that affect revenue.

Using Smart Tags to Enhance Pipeline Organization

While stage colors organize by sales process, Smart Tags

Smart Tags complement color-coded stages by highlighting specific deals within stages. For example:

  • A deal in the "Negotiation" stage that's been sitting there for 30+ days gets tagged as "Stale Deal" (red tag)
  • A deal with deal value over $50,000 gets tagged as "High Priority" (gold tag)
  • A deal where the prospect hasn't responded in 5 days gets tagged as "Follow-Up Needed" (orange tag)

Together, stage colors + Smart Tags create a three-tier visual system: stage (process position), color (stage category), and tags (specific action needed). Your team sees at a glance not just where a deal is, but what action it requires.

This layered organization is what separates agencies running chaotic pipelines from those operating like well-oiled machines.
